chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Programming Languages. (3-0). Credit 3. Exploration of the design space of programming languages via an in-depth study of two programming languages, one functional and one object-oriented; focuses on idiomatic uses of each language and on features characteristic for each language. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Additional Course Details
chunks.content:
This course explores the design space of programming languages via an in-depth study of the most common features and constructs in programming languages, using two programming languages to illustrate them: Haskell (Functional) and Java (object-oriented).

The course focuses on idiomatic uses of functional and object-oriented languages and on feature characteristics of those categories of languages. This course goes into details of how programs written in high-level programming languages are executed.

This includes topics such as static and dynamic environments, internal program representations, type checking, and interpreting. Students will learn about the implementation approaches of common abstraction mechanisms and modern programming language constructs. The course explores the use of the functional programming approach to design and implement programs, as well as study the main features of object-oriented languages.

Although the course uses and studies the above specific programming languages, learning these languages should be considered as a side benefit of the course, not the main goal.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:

• Use modern programming languages effectively
• Develop the necessary skills to learn and adopt new programming languages
• Evaluate the power of programming languages and their constructs
• Apply language constructs, common abstraction mechanisms, and efficiency considerations
• Analyze the meaning and efficiency of functional and object-oriented programs
• Master many specific language concepts and use them effectively to build correct and elegant software

Although the course uses and studies specific programming languages, learning these languages should be considered as a side benefit of the course, not the main goal.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
No Textbook Needed
Notes:
Required textbooks and reading materials will be provided electronically through Perusall and linked on Canvas.

To receive credit, each and all individual Perusall reading assignments must be clicked on in Canvas. The grades will not transfer otherwise.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
CSCE 314 - Grading Policy

| Grade Components | Standard Grading Scale |
| :--------------- | :--------------------- |
| Homework: 10% | A: ≥ 90% |
| In-Class Assignments: 15% | B: ≥ 80% and < 90% |
| Final Project: 15% | C: ≥ 70% and < 80% |
| Midterm Exam: 25% | D: ≥ 60% and < 70% |
| Final Exam: 35% | F: < 60% |

chunks.title: Regrading
chunks.content:
Regrade requests may be made up to one week after grades are published. You must include justification for the change.

We are here to help, but it is the student's responsibility to track progress and seek help before falling behind.

chunks.title: Class Participation
chunks.content: Participation is expected. In-class assignments that demonstrate understanding of the presented topics are counted as part of your grade.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late work is generally not accepted without an excused absence.

It is your responsibility to let us know if you missed something and what date you can have it completed by.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Overview

• Functional programming. In the weeks before fall break, we will work on Functional programming using Haskell. There will be weekly assignments.
• Midterm exam, Wednesday, October 15th, during class.
• Object-oriented programming. In the weeks after Fall break, we will work on OOP using Java. There will be weekly assignments.
• A final project will be due the last week of the semester.
• The final exam is on Thursday, December 11, 10 AM to 12 PM for section 500, and Monday, December 15, 8 to 10 AM for section 501

chunks.title: Major Assignments
chunks.content:
| Item | Focus | Approx.\nCoverage |
| :--- | :---------------------------------------------------- | :---------- |
| HW1 | Functional basics – recursion, ADTs, higher-order functions | Weeks 1–2 |
| HW2 | Polymorphism, laziness, abstraction, infinite structures | Weeks 3–4 |
| HW3 | IO, expression pretty-printer (parsing prep) | Weeks 5–6 |
| Midterm Exam | Covers Haskell & functional programming concepts | Week 8 |
| HW4 | OOP fundamentals – classes, encapsulation, inheritance, exceptions, interfaces | Weeks 9–10 |
| HW5 | Generics, collections, iterators, CSV & I/O utilities | Weeks 11–12 |
| Final Project | JavaFX + MVC app, integrates patterns, persistence, and two pure functional methods in Java | Weeks 13–15 |
| Final Exam | Comprehensive (emphasis on Java/OOP + integration) | Exam Week |

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
The use of AI to gain a greater understanding of a topic is a great use of its capabilities. This is allowed.

The use of AI to do your assignments is a poor use of its capabilities, as it does not increase your capabilities. This is highly discouraged. If done, you must cite the AI tool you used and give the prompts and responses given/received.

There is no access to any form of AI during exams, and historically, those who used AI to complete assignments did poorly on exams.
